title: Quiz: Qubit Calibration Protocols
description: Test your understanding of qubit calibration experiments and procedures

questions.question: In qubit spectroscopy, what determines the linewidth of the observed resonance peak?

options.text: Only the qubit frequency
The drive power (Rabi frequency) and the dephasing rate (1/T2)
Only the T1 relaxation time
The temperature of the dilution refrigerator

questions.explanation: The spectroscopic linewidth is determined by both the drive power (which causes power broadening at high amplitudes) and the natural linewidth from dephasing (1/T2). For accurate frequency determination, low drive power should be used to minimize power broadening.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a Rabi calibration experiment?

options.text: To measure the qubit coherence time T2
To determine the relationship between drive amplitude/duration and rotation angle
To find the qubit resonance frequency
To characterize the readout fidelity

questions.explanation: Rabi calibration determines how the qubit rotation angle depends on the drive amplitude and/or duration. This allows calibration of π and π/2 pulses by finding the correct amplitude or duration for the desired rotation.

questions.question: In a Ramsey experiment, what causes the oscillations in the measured signal?

options.text: Rabi oscillations during the wait time
Detuning between the qubit frequency and the drive frequency
T1 relaxation during the experiment
Thermal fluctuations in the refrigerator

questions.explanation: The Ramsey fringes oscillate at the detuning frequency Δ = ωq - ωd. During the free evolution time τ, the qubit accumulates phase at rate Δ, which converts to a population oscillation after the second π/2 pulse. This is why Ramsey experiments are used for precise frequency calibration.

questions.question: Why is artificial detuning often added in Ramsey experiments?

options.text: To increase the measurement signal strength
To distinguish between positive and negative detuning and create visible fringes when T2* is long
To reduce the effect of T1 relaxation
To improve the spectroscopic resolution

questions.explanation: Artificial detuning serves two purposes: (1) it breaks the symmetry between positive and negative detuning, and (2) when T2* is very long, the natural detuning might be small, making fringes hard to observe. Adding known artificial detuning ensures visible oscillations.

questions.question: What is the relationship between T1, T2, and T2* coherence times?

options.text: T1 = T2 = T2* always
T2* ≤ T2 ≤ 2T1, where T2* includes low-frequency noise effects
T1 < T2 < T2* always
T2* > T2 > T1 always

questions.explanation: The hierarchy is T2* ≤ T2 ≤ 2T1. T2* (Ramsey decay) is limited by both T2 dephasing and low-frequency noise. T2 (Hahn echo) removes low-frequency contributions. The fundamental limit T2 ≤ 2T1 comes from energy relaxation contributing to dephasing.

questions.question: In a T1 measurement, why does the signal decay to a small but non-zero value at long times?

options.text: Measurement errors cause a constant offset
Thermal population of the excited state at finite temperature, plus readout errors
The qubit frequency drifts during the experiment
The drive pulse is not perfectly calibrated

questions.explanation: At finite temperature (even millikelvin), there is a small equilibrium excited state population from thermal excitation. Additionally, readout errors (state preparation and measurement errors) contribute to a non-zero floor in the measured P(|1⟩).

questions.question: What is the correct order for the basic calibration sequence?

options.text: Rabi → Spectroscopy → Ramsey → T1
Spectroscopy → Rabi → Ramsey → T1
Ramsey → Spectroscopy → T1 → Rabi
T1 → T2 → Spectroscopy → Rabi

questions.explanation: The logical order is: (1) Spectroscopy to find the approximate qubit frequency, (2) Rabi to calibrate π and π/2 pulses at that frequency, (3) Ramsey to fine-tune the frequency (requires calibrated π/2 pulses), (4) T1/T2 measurements (require calibrated pulses).

questions.question: A Hahn echo sequence (π/2 - τ/2 - π - τ/2 - π/2) refocuses dephasing from quasi-static frequency fluctuations, resulting in T2 > T2*.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True! The π pulse in a Hahn echo reverses the phase accumulation from quasi-static (low-frequency) noise, causing it to refocus at the end of the sequence. This removes the low-frequency noise contribution, so the measured T2 is longer than T2* which includes all dephasing sources.
